characters.blurb: Athenian of decent but middling house; Themistocles’s hunger for greatness began in that modest shadow.
Invaded Greece with a vast host. Themistocles’s messages and timing at Salamis drew the Persian fleet into the narrows.
Ostracized amid Themistocles’s rise, then recalled against Xerxes; at Salamis the old enemies worked as one.

characters.relation: Spartan admiral of the allied fleet
characters.blurb: Nominal commander at Artemisium and Salamis; Themistocles managed him with argument, threats, and bribes.
Victor of Plataea whose Medism and arrogance later tainted Sparta—and entangled Themistocles in suspicion.
Rose as Themistocles faded; more acceptable to the aristocrats, he inherited Athens’s war against Persia.
His glory at Marathon was the standard Themistocles burned to surpass by making Athens a sea power.

characters.relation: Persian court figure in the exile tale
characters.blurb: Named in the intrigue around Themistocles’s reception in Asia after his flight from Greece.

characters.relation: Trusted servant; false message to Xerxes
characters.blurb: Pedagogue sent to tell Xerxes the Greeks were trapped and ready to flee—so the King would fight in the straits.
Former enemy who received Themistocles as a suppliant at the hearth when Athens and Sparta hunted him.

characters.relation: Host on the road to the King
characters.blurb: Aeolian who hid Themistocles and conveyed him inland toward the Persian court in a closed litter.
Abusive lyric poet who lampooned Themistocles as corrupt and Medizing—a voice of the hate he left behind.
Cean poet who shared Themistocles’s company; Plutarch uses their exchanges to show the general’s wit and vanity.
Named among Themistocles’s children; her marriages mark how his house spread even in exile’s shadow.
Ambitious man Themistocles bought off so he would not hold a generalship in the Persian crisis.
Commander Themistocles coaxed (and paid) to stay and fight when the Greek fleet wavered.
Xerxes’s lieutenant who stayed to finish the land war after Salamis; background to Plataea’s later victory.
Thasian writer Plutarch quotes for anecdotes of Themistocles, Cimon, and Pericles.
Mentioned as the statesman who inherited the naval Athens Themistocles had forced into being.
Appears in a story illustrating Themistocles’s sharp tongue among foreign princes.
Eresian author cited for details of Themistocles’s reception in Persia and death.
Praised Themistocles’s foresight—especially the Piraeus—more coolly than comic poets had.
Among later writers who weighed Themistocles’s cleverness against Aristides’s virtue.
Asian Greek city from which Themistocles drew bread, meat, and wine in the King’s grant—gilded exile.
